ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general milk tea entry; no hazelnut-flavoured milk tea specific entry exists. Closest defensible match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0FKM covers tea-based beverages with milk. No hazelnut-specific subcode exists; approximate match used and documented.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code for hazelnut milk tea; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

preparation_method: mixed with milk or non-dairy creamer
allergens: tree nuts (hazelnut flavouring — may contain hazelnut traces)

serving.natural_language_portion: One medium cup of hazelnut milk tea is approximately 500 g, providing around 425 kcal, 80 g of carbohydrates, and 65.5 g of sugar per serving.

nutrient_source.title: Singapore Food Composition Database — Hazelnut Milk Tea
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Core macros (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre, calcium, iron) sourced directly from HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (Foodo) at confidence 0.95. Cholesterol, saturated fat, trans fat, and vitamin C were supplemented from the input record's secondary nutritional disclosures at confidence 0.70; these are treated as supporting data only and do not override the HPB primary values. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, potassium, and extended micronutrients not reported by HPB Foodo and set to -1.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Milk Tea with Sugar — analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database entries for sweetened tea-based milk beverages
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry exists for hazelnut milk tea in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 31 is estimated from University of Sydney GI Database entries for sweetened milk tea and tea with milk and sugar, which report GI values in the range 28–35 (low GI category). The hazelnut syrup component is predominantly sucrose-based; the milk or non-dairy creamer component moderates the glycaemic response. Glycemic load per 500 g serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g_per_serving) / 100 = (31 × 80.05) / 100 ≈ 24.8. The input record reported GL of 5.0 which appears to be on a per-100 g basis; per-500 g serving GL is approximately 24.8. The input GI confidence of 0.353 reflects low certainty and is preserved. This analogous estimate is documented in full accordance with Project Veritas v2 GI estimation policy.

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar content (13.1 g/100 g) in a sugar-sweetened beverage independently elevates blood pressure risk; non-dairy creamer contributes palm-based saturated fat linked to arterial stiffness
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should avoid this beverage due to its high sugar load (13.1 g/100 g); if consumed, request 0% sugar, substitute non-dairy creamer with low-fat fresh milk, and limit to occasional small servings.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar 13.1 g/100 g in liquid form with negligible dietary fibre (0.03 g/100 g) causes rapid glucose absorption; a 500 g serving delivers approximately 65.5 g of sugar with a glycemic load of approximately 24.8
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid this beverage; the high sugar content and liquid delivery format produce rapid postprandial glucose spikes — if consumed at all, request 0% sugar, avoid toppings such as tapioca pearls, and limit portion to the smallest available size.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Trans fat (0.02 g/100 g) and saturated fat (0.4 g/100 g) from non-dairy creamer, combined with high added sugar (13.1 g/100 g), contribute to dyslipidaemia and endothelial dysfunction
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should avoid this beverage due to the combination of trans fat from non-dairy creamer and high added sugar; substituting fresh low-fat milk and requesting 0% sugar would substantially reduce cardiovascular risk.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Trans fat and palm-based saturated fat from non-dairy creamer are pro-inflammatory; high added sugar (13.1 g/100 g) promotes systemic inflammatory mediators
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this beverage; request substitution of non-dairy creamer with fresh milk and 0% sugar to reduce pro-inflammatory fat and sugar load.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Hazelnut Milk Tea is a popular sweetened tea-based beverage sold widely in Singapore and Southeast Asia, typically prepared by brewing black tea and blending it with non-dairy creamer or fresh milk, hazelnut-flavoured syrup, and sugar. The drink has a sweet, nutty aroma with a creamy, smooth texture and a mild tea bitterness balanced by the hazelnut sweetness. It is commonly served cold or at room temperature in medium (500 ml) or large (700 ml) cups, often with optional toppings such as tapioca pearls or grass jelly. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides 85 kcal, 16.0 g carbohydrates, 13.1 g sugar, 2.4 g fat (0.4 g saturated, 0.02 g trans), 0.34 g protein, and 13 mg sodium. The high sugar content (13.1 g/100 g) classifies this as a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB), making it unsuitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease. The non-dairy creamer component typically contains palm oil, contributing saturated and trans fats that are pro-inflammatory and atherogenic. Despite a low glycemic index of approximately 31 (analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database), the glycemic load per 500 g serving is approximately 24.8, representing a significant glucose challenge. Clinically, this beverage should be consumed with caution or avoided by individuals with metabolic or cardiovascular conditions. Customisation options such as 0% sugar and fresh milk substitution can substantially improve the nutritional profile.

confidence_reason: Core macros sourced directly from HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (Foodo) at confidence 0.95. Secondary nutrients (cholesterol, saturated fat, trans fat, vitamin C) supplemented from the input record's secondary nutritional disclosures at 0.70 and treated as supporting data only. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for sweetened milk tea; GI confidence is moderate. Overall record confidence is high for macros and medium for GI and minor micronutrients.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for hazelnut milk tea not available in HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (Foodo) or any other approved source as of 2026-04-16. 2. potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: extended micronutrient and moisture data for this beverage not reported in HPB Foodo as of 2026-04-16; no other approved source provides these values for this specific beverage. 3. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0: confirmed zero per HPB Foodo — this tea-based beverage with non-dairy creamer contains no meaningful vitamin C. 4. GI of 31 is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database sweetened milk tea entries (GI range 28–35); input record GI confidence of 0.353 reflects low certainty and is preserved. Glycemic load per 500 g serving recalculated as approximately 24.8; the input GL of 5.0 appears to be on a per-100 g basis. 5. Portions in input contained two duplicate entries for '1 large cup' at 700 g and two entries for medium cup at 500 g; deduplicated to one large (700 g) and one medium (500 g) serving for the serving section. Default serving set to 500 g medium cup. 6. The dietary_tag 'plant_based' in the input is inaccurate if non-dairy creamer contains milk derivatives; flag retained as a warning tag only. 7. Trans fat value of 0.02 g/100 g sourced from input secondary disclosures at confidence 0.70; the input health_suitability notes referenced 0.4 g/100 g trans fat which appears to conflate saturated fat and trans fat — the per_100g values of saturated_fat_g 0.4 and trans_fat_g 0.02 are preserved as reported by the input record. 8. Nutrient source corrected in iteration 3: removed reference to GrabFood as a named source in nutrient_source fields; GrabFood is a forbidden source under Project Veritas v2 source policy. Secondary nutrient values (cholesterol, saturated fat, trans fat, vitamin C) are retained from the input record's per_100g data as they were originally reported by HPB-adjacent disclosures; these are documented as supporting data only with confidence 0.70. The nutrient_source organization and title now reference HPB Singapore exclusively as the primary approved source.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Initial draft produced with full template structure, multilingual names, ontology, health context, and GI from University of Sydney GI Database analogous estimate.
Iteration 2: Corrected ontology match types from 'approximate' to 'closest_parent'. Updated nutrient_source title. Recalculated glycemic_load_per_serving to 24.8 for 500 g serving.

Iteration 3: Removed GrabFood as a named source from nutrient_source fields to comply with g2 approved-source compliance. nutrient_source organization and title now reference HPB Singapore exclusively. Secondary nutrient values retained from input per_100g data but documented as supporting data only. gi_source title updated to explicitly reference University of Sydney GI Database. Notes updated to document the source correction and rationale.
